mcp.definition: A protocol that allows AI models to connect to external data sources and tools. Think of it as a universal adapter that lets AI assistants access your files, databases, and APIs safely.

unix.definition: A family of operating systems known for simplicity and portability. Core philosophy: everything is a file, small tools that do one thing well, and combine via pipes.

ci.definition: Automated testing that runs every time you push code. Catches bugs early by running tests, linters, and builds automatically.

cd.definition: Automates releasing code to production after tests pass. CD = ship confident code faster.

docker.definition: Packages applications with their dependencies into containers. Like a lightweight VM - runs the same everywhere, no 'works on my machine' issues.

kubernetes.definition: Orchestrates Docker containers at scale. Handles deployment, scaling, and management. Think: conductor for a container orchestra.

api.definition: A contract for software to communicate. Like a restaurant menu - tells you what you can order and how.

rest.definition: An API design style using HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE). Stateless, cacheable, and widely adopted.

grpc.definition: High-performance RPC framework using HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers. Faster than REST for service-to-service communication.

git.definition: Distributed version control system. Tracks changes, enables collaboration, and saves you from 'oops' moments.

ssh.definition: Protocol for secure remote access to servers. Encrypts everything, uses key-based auth.

jwt.definition: Compact, self-contained tokens for auth. Contains encoded data (not encrypted) - sign but don't store secrets in them.

oauth.definition: Authorization framework. Lets apps access your data without giving them your password. Used by 'Login with Google/GitHub'.

sdk.definition: Pre-built tools and libraries for a platform. Speeds up development by providing ready-to-use code.

ide.definition: Code editor on steroids. Combines writing, debugging, and building code. Examples: VS Code, IntelliJ, PyCharm.

linter.definition: Static analysis tool that finds bugs and style issues before you run code. Like spellcheck for your code.

yaml.definition: Human-readable data format. Uses indentation (spaces, not tabs!). Common for config files like Kubernetes, GitHub Actions.

json.definition: Lightweight data interchange format. Easy for humans to read, easy for machines to parse. The lingua franca of APIs.

orm.definition: Lets you interact with databases using objects instead of SQL. Examples: SQLAlchemy, Prisma, Hibernate.

sql.definition: Language for interacting with relational databases. SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE - the basics of data manipulation.

nosql.definition: Non-relational databases for flexible schemas. Good for unstructured data. Examples: MongoDB, Redis, Cassandra.

microservices.definition: Architecture pattern: small, independent services. Each does one thing, communicates via APIs. Opposite of monolith.

monolith.definition: Single unified codebase for all functionality. Simpler to develop and deploy, but harder to scale independently.

serverless.definition: Cloud runs your code without managing servers. Pay per execution. Examples: AWS Lambda, Cloudflare Workers.

websocket.definition: Protocol for real-time, bidirectional communication. Unlike HTTP, the connection stays open. Perfect for chat apps, live updates, and gaming.

cors.definition: Browser security feature. Controls which domains can access your API. Misconfigure it = lots of red console errors.

csrf.definition: Attack that tricks users into submitting unwanted actions. Prevented with CSRF tokens and SameSite cookies.

xss.definition: Attack injecting malicious scripts into web pages. Prevent by escaping user input and using Content Security Policy.

env.definition: File storing environment variables (API keys, config). NEVER commit to git. Use .env.example for templates.

mutex.definition: Locking mechanism preventing race conditions. Only one thread can access a resource at a time.

race_condition.definition: Bug where outcome depends on timing. Multiple processes access shared data = unpredictable results.

idempotent.definition: Operation that gives same result no matter how many times called. GET, PUT should be idempotent. POST is not.

async.definition: Pattern for handling asynchronous operations. Write async code that looks synchronous. Non-blocking, efficient.

callback.definition: Function passed as argument, called when operation completes. Foundation of async JS, can lead to 'callback hell'.

promise.definition: Object representing future value. States: pending, fulfilled, rejected. Cleaner than callbacks for async code.

functional_programming.definition: Paradigm using pure functions, immutability. No side effects, no shared state. map/filter/reduce are your friends.

oop.definition: Paradigm using objects with data and behavior. Key concepts: encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism.

solid.definition: 5 design principles: Single responsibility, Open/closed, Liskov substitution, Interface segregation, Dependency inversion.

dry.definition: Principle: every piece of knowledge should have one authoritative representation. Reduce duplication.

kiss.definition: Principle: simplicity should be a key goal. Avoid unnecessary complexity. Complex solutions create complex bugs.

yagni.definition: Principle: don't build features you don't need yet. Save time, reduce complexity.

tech_debt.definition: Shortcuts that speed up now but slow you later. Like financial debt: sometimes useful, always needs repayment.

refactoring.definition: Restructuring code without changing behavior. Improve readability, reduce complexity. Do it in small steps.

tdd.definition: Write tests before code. Cycle: write failing test, make it pass, refactor. Ensures testable, well-designed code.

mocking.definition: Creating fake objects for testing. Isolate unit tests from dependencies like databases, APIs.

deprecation.definition: Marking code as obsolete. Still works, but will be removed. Signals: migrate to newer alternative.

semantic_versioning.definition: Versioning scheme: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. Breaking changes bump major, features bump minor, fixes bump patch.

hash.definition: Fixed-size output from arbitrary input. Same input = same hash. Used for passwords, data integrity, caching.

encryption.definition: Converting data into secret code. Symmetric = same key encrypt/decrypt. Asymmetric = public/private key pair.

base64.definition: Encoding binary data as ASCII text. Not encryption! Used for images in HTML, email attachments.

dns.definition: Translates domain names to IP addresses. The internet's phonebook. Example: google.com -> 142.250.185.78

load_balancer.definition: Distributes traffic across multiple servers. Prevents overload, enables zero-downtime deployments.

reverse_proxy.definition: Server that forwards client requests to backend servers. Nginx, HAProxy are common examples.

cache.definition: Temporary storage for fast access. Trade-off: speed vs freshness. Examples: Redis, Memcached, browser cache.

cdn.definition: Distributed servers delivering content from nearest location. Speeds up static assets globally.

blue_green.definition: Two identical environments. Switch traffic from old (blue) to new (green). Enables instant rollback.

canary_deployment.definition: Roll out changes to small subset first. If issues detected, blast radius is limited. Named after canary in coal mine.

feature_flag.definition: Toggle features on/off without deploying. Enables gradual rollouts, A/B testing, quick rollbacks.

observability.definition: Understanding system state from external outputs. Three pillars: logs, metrics, traces.

logging.definition: Recording events for debugging and auditing. Levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR. Don't log secrets!

metrics.definition: Numerical measurements over time. Track performance, errors, usage. Prometheus is the standard.

tracing.definition: Track requests across microservices. Debug latency, find bottlenecks. Tools: Jaeger, Zipkin.

slo.definition: Target reliability metric. Example: 99.9% uptime. Defines what 'good' looks like.

sla.definition: Contract specifying reliability commitments. Miss the SLA = financial penalties or credits.

error_budget.definition: Allowed amount of unreliability. If SLO is 99.9%, you have 43.2 min downtime/month. Spend it wisely.

iac.definition: Managing infrastructure via code instead of manual processes. Reproducible, version-controlled, auditable. Tools: Terraform, Pulumi, CloudFormation.

terraform.definition: HashiCorp's IaC tool. Declarative config files describe desired infrastructure state. Plan before apply = no surprises.

immutable_infrastructure.definition: Never modify running servers - replace them. Eliminates config drift, makes rollbacks trivial.

event_driven.definition: Services communicate by emitting and consuming events. Decoupled, scalable. Examples: Kafka, SQS, EventBridge.

message_queue.definition: Buffer between producers and consumers. Decouples services, handles traffic spikes. Examples: RabbitMQ, SQS, Kafka.

pub_sub.definition: Messaging pattern: publishers emit events, subscribers receive them. Many-to-many, decoupled. Examples: Kafka, Google Pub/Sub, SNS.

saga.definition: Manages distributed transactions across microservices. Chain of local transactions with compensating rollbacks on failure.

cqrs.definition: Separate read and write models. Commands mutate state, queries read it. Enables independent scaling of reads and writes.

event_sourcing.definition: Store state as a sequence of events, not current values. Replay events to rebuild state. Full audit trail, time travel debugging.

circuit_breaker.definition: Stops calling a failing service to prevent cascade failures. States: closed (normal), open (failing), half-open (testing recovery).

backpressure.definition: Signal from consumer to producer to slow down. Prevents overwhelming downstream services. Key concept in reactive systems.

rate_limiting.definition: Restricts how many requests a client can make in a time window. Protects APIs from abuse and overload.

throttling.definition: Slowing down requests rather than rejecting them. Gentler than rate limiting - queues excess requests instead of dropping them.

sharding.definition: Horizontal partitioning of a database. Each shard holds a subset of data. Scales writes beyond a single node.

replication.definition: Copying data across multiple nodes. Primary handles writes, replicas handle reads. Improves availability and read throughput.

cap_theorem.definition: Distributed systems can guarantee only 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance. Network partitions are inevitable, so choose CA or CP.

eventual_consistency.definition: Distributed systems guarantee data will converge to same value - eventually. Trades immediate consistency for availability.

acid.definition: Database transaction properties: Atomicity (all or nothing), Consistency (valid state), Isolation (concurrent safety), Durability (persisted).

index.definition: Data structure speeding up queries at the cost of write overhead and storage. Like a book's index - fast lookup, extra maintenance.

n_plus_one.definition: Fetching a list then querying each item individually. 1 query + N queries = slow. Fix with eager loading or JOIN.

connection_pool.definition: Reuses database connections instead of creating new ones per request. Reduces latency and DB overhead.

garbage_collection.definition: Automatic memory management. Runtime reclaims unused memory. Trades manual control for safety. GC pauses can affect latency.

heap.definition: Memory region for dynamic allocation. Objects live here. Managed by GC in most languages. Opposite of stack.

stack.definition: LIFO memory for function calls and local variables. Fast, auto-managed. Limited size - overflow it and you get a stack overflow.

deadlock.definition: Two or more processes waiting on each other forever. Process A holds lock X, wants Y. Process B holds Y, wants X. Neither proceeds.

semaphore.definition: Signaling mechanism controlling access to a resource pool. Like a mutex but allows N concurrent holders instead of 1.

goroutine.definition: Go's lightweight concurrent function. Cheaper than OS threads - millions can run simultaneously. Communicate via channels.

channel.definition: Go's typed pipe for goroutine communication. 'Don't communicate by sharing memory; share memory by communicating.'

closure.definition: Function that captures variables from its enclosing scope. The function 'closes over' those variables.

currying.definition: Transforming a multi-argument function into a chain of single-argument functions. f(a, b) becomes f(a)(b).

memoization.definition: Caching function results for given inputs. Call with same args = return cached result. Trades memory for speed.

big_o.definition: Describes algorithm complexity as input grows. O(1) constant, O(log n) logarithmic, O(n) linear, O(n²) quadratic.

binary_search.definition: Find item in sorted list by halving search space each step. O(log n). Requires sorted input.

recursion.definition: Function that calls itself. Needs a base case to stop. Elegant for tree/graph problems. Watch for stack overflow.

dependency_injection.definition: Pass dependencies in rather than creating them inside. Decouples code, makes testing easy. Core to SOLID's D principle.

middleware.definition: Code that runs between request and response. Handles auth, logging, rate limiting. Composable pipeline pattern.

webhook.definition: HTTP callback triggered by an event. Server pushes data to your URL when something happens. Reverse of polling.

polling.definition: Repeatedly asking a server for updates. Simple but inefficient. Replace with webhooks or WebSockets for real-time needs.

long_polling.definition: Client sends request, server holds it open until data is available. Simulates push without WebSockets.

sse.definition: One-way server-to-client streaming over HTTP. Simpler than WebSockets for push-only use cases like live feeds.

graphql.definition: Query language for APIs. Clients request exactly the data they need. Solves over-fetching and under-fetching of REST.

openapi.definition: Specification for describing REST APIs. Machine-readable contract enables auto-generated docs, clients, and mocks.

protobuf.definition: Google's binary serialization format. Smaller and faster than JSON. Requires schema (.proto file). Used with gRPC.

regex.definition: Pattern language for matching text. Powerful but hard to read. Test with regex101.com before shipping.

cron.definition: Unix job scheduler. Cron expression: minute hour day month weekday. '0 2 * * *' = 2am daily.

daemon.definition: Background process running without user interaction. Starts at boot, runs continuously. Examples: nginx, sshd, systemd services.

pid.definition: Unique number assigned to each running process. Use kill <PID> to stop a process. Find with ps or pgrep.

stdin_stdout.definition: Standard streams: stdin (input), stdout (output), stderr (errors). Pipe stdout to next command with |. Redirect with > and 2>.

symlink.definition: File that points to another file or directory. Like a shortcut. ln -s target link_name.

chmod.definition: Change file permissions. chmod 755 = owner rwx, group rx, others rx. chmod +x = make executable.

tls.definition: Cryptographic protocol securing network communication. Successor to SSL. HTTPS = HTTP over TLS.

mtls.definition: Both client and server authenticate with certificates. Used in service meshes and zero-trust networks.

zero_trust.definition: Security model: never trust, always verify. No implicit trust based on network location. Every request authenticated and authorized.

rbac.definition: Permissions assigned to roles, roles assigned to users. Easier to manage than per-user permissions.

oidc.definition: Identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0. OAuth handles authorization; OIDC adds authentication. Returns ID tokens (JWTs).

saml.definition: XML-based standard for SSO. Enterprise staple. Browser redirects between identity provider and service provider.

sso.definition: Log in once, access multiple apps. Implemented via SAML, OIDC, or OAuth. Reduces password fatigue.

wasm.definition: Binary instruction format running in browsers at near-native speed. Run C, Rust, Go in the browser. Also used server-side.

edge_computing.definition: Processing data near the source rather than a central cloud. Lower latency, less bandwidth. Examples: Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge.

service_mesh.definition: Infrastructure layer handling service-to-service communication. Provides mTLS, retries, circuit breaking, observability. Examples: Istio, Linkerd.

sidecar.definition: Deploy helper container alongside main container. Handles cross-cutting concerns: logging, proxying, auth. Used in service meshes.

gitops.definition: Git as single source of truth for infrastructure and deployments. Merge to main = deploy. Tools: ArgoCD, Flux.

trunk_based.definition: Everyone commits to main frequently. Short-lived branches or none. Requires feature flags. Enables true CI.

monorepo.definition: Single repository for multiple projects/packages. Easier code sharing and atomic changes. Tools: Nx, Turborepo, Bazel.

bun.definition: Fast JavaScript runtime, bundler, and package manager. Drop-in Node.js replacement. Written in Zig.

deno.definition: Secure JavaScript/TypeScript runtime by Node.js creator. No node_modules, built-in TypeScript, permission-based security.

lsp.definition: Protocol between editors and language tools. Write one language server, works in all editors. Powers autocomplete, go-to-definition.

ast.definition: Tree representation of source code structure. Used by compilers, linters, and code formatters to analyze and transform code.

transpiler.definition: Compiles source code to another language at similar abstraction level. TypeScript → JavaScript, Babel ES2024 → ES5.

tree_shaking.definition: Dead code elimination in bundlers. Removes unused exports. Requires ES modules (import/export, not require).

hot_reload.definition: Update running app without full restart. Browser reflects code changes instantly. Preserves state. Standard in modern dev servers.
